name: Carey Agate Beds
short_description: BLM public rockhounding at Carey Agate Beds in central Oregon's high desert. Find colorful agates and chalcedony specimens in weathered volcanic terrain with no permit required.
description:
Carey Agate Beds is a popular public rockhounding destination on Bureau of Land Management property in Crook County, Oregon. Located at 6,043 feet elevation in the transition zone between Oregon's Cascade Range and high desert plateau, this site offers excellent opportunities for collectors to find quality agate and chalcedony specimens without permits or fees.

The area sits within Oregon's famous agate and thunderegg country, where Miocene-age volcanic activity and subsequent weathering have exposed numerous silica-rich deposits. The terrain here consists of gently sloping hills with exposed volcanic ash beds and rhyolitic outcrops that weather to reveal agate nodules and specimens. Surface collecting is productive year-round, particularly after rainfall when specimens are freshly exposed.

Visitors can expect to find colorful banded agates in shades of red, orange, white, and cream throughout the weathered volcanic material. The site is family-friendly and beginner-accessible, requiring only hand tools and basic rockhounding skills. The gentle terrain makes it suitable for collectors of varying experience levels.

This location is managed by the BLM under standard recreational collecting guidelines. The high desert elevation means conditions vary seasonally, with spring and fall offering the most comfortable collecting temperatures. Summer heat can be intense at this elevation, while winter snow occasionally affects access.
history: Carey Agate Beds lies within Oregon's Blue Mountains province, where massive Miocene volcanic eruptions deposited ash and silica-rich materials approximately 25 million years ago. Subsequent weathering and erosion of these volcanic deposits concentrated silica into agate nodules that collectors find today. This area has been a known rockhounding destination for decades, with amateur collectors and rockhounding clubs regularly visiting the publicly accessible BLM lands.
tips: Search the weathered slopes and wash areas where erosion has exposed the volcanic ash matrix - look for colorful agate nodules emerging from light-colored volcanic material. Bring a small pry bar or geologist's hammer to carefully extract nodules from the surrounding rock, and a chisel for opening specimens. Early morning and late afternoon light helps reveal the translucent edges of agate nodules in the field. After rain, freshly exposed specimens become visible on the surface, making post-storm visits particularly productive.
terrain_notes: Gentle rolling hills at 6,043 feet elevation covered with sagebrush and scattered juniper. The ground surface is a mix of volcanic ash, loose rock, and exposed outcrops. Walking is relatively easy on the gentle slopes, though footing can be uneven in places. Most productive collecting areas are within a short walk of parking. The volcanic material is stable underfoot but loose rock is common.
written_directions: From Prineville, take US 26 east toward John Day for approximately 18 miles. The Carey Agate Beds area lies in the rolling terrain north of the highway in the vicinity of Carey. Look for informal pullouts and parking areas on or near BLM land accessed via local roads and ranch roads in the area. A detailed map or GPS coordinates are helpful as roads and access points can be informal.
road_conditions: Gravel and dirt ranch roads provide access. Road surface varies from well-maintained gravel to rougher tracks. High-clearance vehicles may be beneficial after rain, but typical passenger vehicles can access main areas during dry conditions.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas scattered throughout the BLM land. No designated lot, but multiple spots available for vehicles. Capacity varies by location.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Pack out all waste. Respect private property boundaries that may adjoin this BLM land.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Pack out all waste
Respect private property boundaries
nearest_services: Prineville, approximately 20 miles west, has gas, food, lodging, and supplies
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and collectors with basic mobility. Uneven volcanic terrain and loose rock present throughout. Sturdy footwear recommended. No developed trails or wheelchair access. Informal parking and walking directly from vehicles to collecting areas.

faq.question: What types of agates can I find at Carey Agate Beds in Oregon?
faq.answer: Carey Agate Beds is known for colorful banded agates in shades of red, orange, white, and cream, along with chalcedony specimens. Many collectors find agates with distinctive patterns and quality suitable for cabochons. Surface specimens range from small nodules to larger pieces, with translucent sections particularly valued.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Carey Agate Beds?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting at Carey Agate Beds. The site is on BLM public land where personal use rockhounding is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day at no cost.

faq.question: What is the best time to visit Carey Agate Beds for rockhounding?
faq.answer: October through April offers the most comfortable collecting conditions, with moderate temperatures and less intense sun. Spring and fall are ideal for day trips. Summer heat can be intense at this 6,043-foot elevation, while winter may bring snow. Post-rain visits often yield freshly exposed specimens.

faq.question: Is Carey Agate Beds suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Carey Agate Beds is beginner-friendly with easy, gently sloping terrain that works well for families and children ages 6 and up. Surface collecting requires only basic hand tools like a small hammer or pry bar. No special skills or experience is needed to find specimens.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to Carey Agate Beds?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar, geologist's hammer, or chisel for extracting nodules. Pack plenty of water, sunscreen, and a hat for sun protection in the high desert. A field bag, GPS unit or phone with downloaded maps, and sturdy hiking boots are essential. Bring bags for collecting your 25 lb daily limit.

faq.question: How far is Carey Agate Beds from Prineville, Oregon?
faq.answer: Carey Agate Beds is approximately 20 miles east of Prineville. Take US 26 toward John Day and look for informal access areas on BLM land in the rolling hills north of the highway. Prineville has all services including gas, food, and lodging for rockhounders.

meta_description: Find colorful agates and chalcedony at Carey Agate Beds. BLM public rockhounding near Prineville. No permit required, 25 lbs per day. Family-friendly.
